text:
I don’t have time for waste management—is it really about time or just priorities?

An average Indian spends 3-4 hours daily on social media. We find time to debate politics, cricket, and entertainment, track celebrity news, and stay updated on reality shows—but when it comes to managing our own waste, we say we’re too busy?  

In Japan, one of the world's most hardworking nations, people still take time to segregate waste at home. Even in Doraemon, Nobita is shown cleaning his school—because responsibility and discipline are taught from a young age.

Waste management isn’t just the government’s duty—it’s a collective responsibility. A cleaner environment starts with small, mindful actions at home.  

So, before saying "I don’t have time," take a moment to check your screen time—and rethink what truly matters.

text:
Lots of inaccuracies in her opening statement. 

Firstly, Venus is less than 70 million miles from the Sun and Earth is on average around 93 million miles from the Sun. Carbon, in Earth's atmosphere is comprised of around 0.042% Carbon Dioxide whilst Venus comprises 95% Carbon Dioxide.

Mercury doesn't have an atmosphere so heat can't ever be trapped on the planet so if there is nothing to heat, then it merely is the temperature of the Sun's rays as they reach the planet surface, on Mercury. Mercury's temperature varies a lot, it can be as hot 400 plus Celsius but also really cold on the surface facing away from the Sun. Because the planet rotates very slowly, it's day is longer than it's year so it's average temperature is very reasonable when you average these out as a result of an extremely thin, almost nonexistent atmosphere.

Also, the density of the atmosphere on Venus is 90 times that of the Earth. All these factors have a major impact on what can happen where. The Earth would struggle to get to 0.2% Carbon Dioxide in the atmosphere, due to the volume of life which is carbon based, that is the reality regardless of how much carbon was ever released from the Earth. If all life was vapourised on Earth, what would happen, who can say. Humans cause more damage trying to fix things than letting nature take it's course. They destroy perfectly balanced ecosystems and plant palm oil plantations for biofuels. We are cause and effect of all things wasteful and it is refuse which is the Greatest threat to our planet along with trying to save it. We must produce less waste and travel less.

Speaking of which, I only do necessary journeys and most of my travel is by cycle, unlike the politicians that claim to want to save the planet and tell us all to use our cars less and don't travel.

Meanwhile, they travel all over the World without impunity, expecting to be heard, despite being two-faced Vogons. You might want to check out The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy.

text:
As a Pacific islander i cried. My ancestors been living on these islands for thousands of years. I come from an island just a few feet above sea level. We rely 100% on what we grow. Our high tides are becoming more frequent & more damaging. The taro is our main staple food along with breadfruit & bananas, but now most of our taro patches are becoming salinated because salt water is seeping into our underground water lenses making our only main source of fresh water salty. Fewer fish is also a major issue. Long droughts & frequent storms killing people, livestock & destroying farms. 
I thank this young women for speaking on such issue. ❤️

text:
Little things you can do to help:

Pull the cord out of the socket once you are done using the device. 
I.e If you’ve been charging your phone, pull the cord out when it’s fully charged.

Lower your consumption of meat products.
The absolute ideal is to get rid of all meat, but if you can’t because of various reasons then I recommend: Avoid eating red meat(lamb, beef, pork etc), and if you were to eat it, buy the meat from a local farm or check where the meat is from. Have a varied diet, make some days of your week vegetarian or vegan. Many schools in my country have at least one day of only vegan food. 

Avoid fast fashion.
Fast fashion is what you call mass production of clothing, often in bad working environments and extremely bad for the climate. Two very huge fast fashion brands are H&M and Zara. When buying clothing check with the store/brand on where the clothes are produced. The best option to avoid fast fashion is to get hand-me-downs or shop at thrift stores, which actually seems to be gaining popularity with the younger generations.

Buy only what you need.
Think hardly before buying anything: Do I really need this? Is this really necessary or is it just because of aesthetic, self-satisfactory or popularity reasons?

Stop using plasting bags and other single-use plastics.
A plastic bag has an extremely short lifespan, mostly only lasting from the shop and then to your home. There are many other safer options than plastic bags such as regular bags, backpacks or tote bags(which also seem to be getting popular). Single-use plastics can range from anything such as plastic straws, cutlery, candy wrappers to plates. Replace that plastic straw with one made out of a reusable material like bamboo or metal, please reduce your consumption of candy and bring cutlery with you if needed.

Public transport.
As uncomfortable as it may be to sit on a bus or a train, it is a better option than to use a private car. More people sharing things means less production of said thing.

Please add to this list if you want to, we need more alternatives to better ourselves and save the planet.
video_title: School strike for climate - save the world by changing the rules | Greta Thunberg | TEDxStockholm

text:
Even three years after her talking about this specific problem it is not enough done by the politicians to save these islanders. Scientists are still concerned about it and not just Greta’s generation but the whole society is still demonstrating every Friday and constantly making people aware of climate change and its consequences. I think it is good to remind each other about how this whole movement once started and follow Greta on her mission to not save the planet, but to try to change people's opinion. To keep doing that and pick up the conversation about it, I would like to ask people that are watching this video even 3, 4 or 5 years after it has been published, what you guys think could be done to make the change possible and what has been done that really improved our situation? 
I'm looking forward to a good conversation that will last for years.

text: Thank you Greta.  I would be very happy to work with students like you to collaboratively design a Climate Science curriculum to help develop more sustainable ways to live.  Then we could build sustainable and non-toxic homes and offices safer for people and our climate than the new homes, schools, and office buildings being built today.  I wish our school administrative leaders would place a higher priority on real Science learning and achievement and support teachers' efforts to help students learn with hands-on Science laboratories and field trips as well as empowering advocacy.  Unfortunately our students and administrators in racially segregated schools are too caught up in the politics of race, resistance, and victimization.  Can we please make sure every student understands the ways our decisions and actions connect to Climate Science so every President from 2020 forward can seriously and authentically engage with and solve our Greenhouse Gas problems?

text:
I live in India. A third world developing country with a huge population of 1.5billion. 
Here, the main concern of people is to find food for the next day. No one cares about climate change or anything. They're going to starve to death if they don't work. That's the only thing that Matters here. 
I hope she one day visits our country and see what is poverty. She did a school strike to support a protest. We reduce food from our plates just to support another year of higher education.
We known what is climate change and how it's destroying the world. But it's easy when you're from a wealthy developed country. I'm 17 and I can't buy a new pair of clothes, not before 2 months. And here you are asking me to place solar panels on my house.

text:
Assuming she isn't brainwashed. Anyone can stand up and memorise scientific facts and say "there needs to be change". You have to provide proper solutions on how you can transition from non-renewable sources of energy to renewables gracefully in a way that won't hurt the economy. If radical change takes fold then the economy suffers, as a result people's livelihood's are lost.

Also making ordinary people feel guilty about climate change isn't a smart way to lead change. The ordinary folks aren't the ones who decide how the economy is run, they have more short-term priorities such as putting food on the table, ensure their mortgage gets paid off and taking care of their families. Not everyone is privileged as Greta who has the means to do what they are doing. Sure we can all do the small things like recycle where possible but our actions are pointless in relation to the actions of those running nations and corporations. 

On top of that, the whole climate change movement has been politicized only to hurt the ordinary people by getting them to pay higher taxes in the name of "helping change the climate". I can't side with something turned into a gigantic scam.

text:
Excellent and very thoughtful question 🌱 — this shows you’re thinking deeply about how Jain principles apply in modern farming methods.

Let’s look at it carefully from a Jain philosophical point of view 👇

⸻

🥔 The core principle still applies — not about where it grows, but what it is.

Even if potatoes (or other root vegetables) are grown in vertical farms, aeroponics, or hydroponics — meaning they don’t grow in soil or require digging — they are still root vegetables.
From the Jain point of view, that means:
	1.	The edible part is the root itself — eating it still involves destroying the entire plant.
	2.	The plant has “anantkaya” (many souls within one body), according to Jain scriptures.
	3.	Therefore, consuming it is himsa (violence) at the level of plant life.

⸻

💧 What about “non-soil” or “ethical” potato farming?

Even in soil-free systems:
	•	The root system is still the life-base of the plant.
	•	Harvesting means killing the whole organism, even if no soil organisms are harmed.
So, while it reduces physical violence (no worms or soil microbes are killed), it doesn’t remove the spiritual and biological violence toward the plant itself.

⸻

🧘 Jain ethical view

Jainism’s dietary rules are principle-based, not purely practical.
The intent is to minimize all forms of violence, whether visible or invisible.
So even with advanced farming methods, Jains who strictly follow ahimsa would still avoid potatoes and all underground/root vegetables, no matter how they are grown.

text:
There's an even more fundamental issue not being addressed...

According to the US EIA, the current road-going US transportation fleet (all cars/trucks), uses about 200 Billion GGE (Gasoline Gallon Equivalent) per year.

1 GGE = about 33.5 Kilowatt-Hours

200,000,000,000 GGE  x  33.5kWh = 6,700,000,000,000kWh.

6,700,000,000,000kWh is the same as 6,700,000,000 Megawatt-Hours, or 
6,700,000 Gigawatt-Hours, or 
6,700 Terawatt-Hours, or
6.7 Petawatt-Hours.

Electric vehicles are far more efficient than ICE vehicles, so multiple that by 0.25 to get an approximate figure for the power needed, and that gives us 1.675 Petawatt-Hours. 

This assumes 100% efficiency through the entire grid of course, with no line loss or other issues.

And THAT is at a bare minimum, the additional baseload that we would need to generate to convert to an all-electric fleet.

For perspective, we are currently using 4 Petawatt-Hours of electricity each year to run the ENTIRE COUNTRY.

Frankly, I have no idea how we're going to meet our increased power needs, which are expected to rise by about 50% by 2040, without building a LOT of nuclear power plants, and that's WITHOUT electric vehicles complicating the issue.

My BEST GUESS is that if we want to have even a majority EV fleet by 2050, we'll need to generate at a MINIMUM, 8 Petawatt-Hours of electricity every year.

text:
This is how to fix Earth.  Read to the end for the surprise because each step interlocks. 
Our only focus in our lives should be to love each other unconditionally.  That is our only hope.  We need to not try to separate ourselves from others that don't look like us or don't have the status, money or opportunities we don't have.  I don't think that we are here on Earth to just be comfortable and only care about our own.  Our lives are basically a test from a bigger power than us.  Don't freak out.  I'm not focusing on religion or anything else.  I'm focusing on homeless people, as well as poor people, as well as so called ugly people, as well as short people, along with any other difference that creates polarization (division) on all races on all continents.  That division we have imposed on ourselves as a species is deeply detrimental to passing the Test we are all taking and most likely failing day by day.  The rich get richer and the poor get more bitter and more desperate which leads to greed, crime, and lack of compassion towards ourselves and others.  You each have the power to change your neighborhoods, your towns, your cities, your country and OUR planet.  But, it's not going to happen thinking only about me and mine.  We have to think about our world and our progress.  If one stumbles, pick him or her up.  Don't let people die on the street from hunger or from an addiction.  Yes it's hard getting involved in curing Earth's problems.  But, by not focusing on eradicating those dysfunctional situations with patience and discipline without violence, we are just helping multiply all social cancers.  I know this comment is long.  But, if you are still reading this is because you know things can change and you have hope.  But, hope is not enough.  Even Aliens are worried about us.  This is the only planet we have.  Yet, all we focus on is destroying it through wars, greed for more money and no urge to walk together as a species.  That is why the world's biggest mistakes and massacres always repeat themselves in all continents and islands.  Lets wake up people.  Lets evolve.  You know how to live to your full potential by helping others and loving all unconditionally.  That's what band The Beatles were all about.  That's what WE can be all about.  One more thought.  Look at how many countries around the world has forced their citizens out of it into other countries such as World War 2 which forced Europeans and Jews into America.  And the conflicts in the Middle East in countries like Yemen, Syria and Israel has forced many into Europe.  Or the problems in Central America and Venezuela such as poverty, gang wars and communist take overs have pushed citizens out of their land to the United States and Europe.  And the war lords killings in Africa has pushed millions out of that continent in search of safety.  If we really pay attention, we will realize all of these tragedies are allowed to happen by a higher power to force us to assimilate with each other.  It's a rough way of that happening.  But, that is the result.  So, there lays the rub.  It's about accepting others and not isolating yourselves from anyone who is not of your race.  That is the current test going on around the world.  That test is more powerful than us.  We are only around about 100 years or less.  This test is ongoing for thousands of years.  Lets drop our ego and lets realize our purpose of existing.  Lets respect, appreciate and honor progress and unity.  Otherwise, one day nothing on Earth will be worth salvaging, including your future descendants and our present idealisms. ❤  Sincerely, Frank (human from Earth).

text: I am very lucky to live in an area with a great air quality. There are lots of green trees providing shade surrounded my hometown, and that’s why people in my neighborhood spend more time exercising every morning to enjoy the fresh air. The local government makes it illegal to throw out garbage on the street and put many public bins to raise local people's awareness about environmental issues. In addition, there aren’t any industrial zones so the environment is less polluted. Local people have a bin in their house and they usually put many kinds of waste in it. Unfortunately, people don't have a habit of sorting the  waste into recyclables and non-recyclables. At the weekend, all of the rubbish will be collected by trash collectors and gone into landfill nearby. To keep the environment clean, local people are encouraged to grow food organically without using pesticides or herbicides.

text:
Part 1: 
Generally, I'd say the air quality is not that good because it can still get hazy in winter. But it's much better thaN it was a decade ago. At that time, you'd get thick smog covering the whole city all the year round.
The improvement was achieved by the efforts of the government. They brought in new laws and regulations, restricted the volume of vehicles in the city center, invested more in public transport infrastructure, and moved a lot of heavy industry away from the city.
As far as I'm concerned, vehicle emission is one of the major causes of air pollution, therefore to combat air pollution it is fundamental to reduce the reliance on private cars by using more public transport and replace gasoline-fueled vehicles with zero emission automobiles such as electric ones.
Part 4: 
For sure, I think it's indeed a big concern, because climate change can lead to a number of environmental issues such as rising sea levels, regional changes in rainfall patterns, and disruption to THE ecosystem. For example, some regions may experience more rain and snowfall, while others may suffer from more frequent droughts and wildfires. These in turn could reduce crop yields and harm food security.
I can imagine my country WILL have more frequent floods and even loss of some land in the coastline if rising sea levels continue.
Global warming is primarily caused by an increase of fossil fuel burning. Therefore it is essential to reduce carbon emission by  using more clean energy such as solar energy, and replacing gasoline-fueled cars with electric ones. One top of that, forest preservation and reforestation programs, which can increase the natural capacity of carbon absorption, would also play an important role in combating this issue.

text:
Well, first of all let me say that your lessons are MY CUP OF TEA!
As far as environment I can say that I do not care about it and I am going to give you my explanation about that. 
So, doesn't matter of ordinary people's attitudes the environment can not be better or worse. The environment issue is in hand of huge companies in the world. Really,  what can I do for the environment? Sure, I can just replace my car diesel for the electric one, sort the garbage front of my house and what else?
In my opinion the environment issue is in the hand of huge companies which abuse all laws and via politicians destroyed our environment. 
As far as my city, air quality can be better. So, our industry do not exist more near of center of town but the traffic must be banned for everyone.  For example, if you allow to come in center of town because the people can pay extra tax, you are on the wrong way. Why, because the rich people do not care about money. It means you banned to come in center just for poor people. The rich people can afford to pay any amount of money, bring their car in the center next to the pub or shop and they are very important unlike poor people. So, it must be banned for everyone, doesn't matter poor or rich people. 
Recyclable, so far we do not any chance to collect garbage on the proper way. Yes, we have some rules about sort of garbage but just minor people respect that. You can see from the building some bins (yellow, blue, black, grey), but sometimes it is funny to se people that they use the green bins for the bio waste. In my hometown we can recycle just a few things like glass and some minor things. The most of the garbage goes to the landfill and burnt. I can not say that we are environmental friendly in my town.  We can find a lot of garbage piles around our town. 
Farming
Farming food, meat, chicken, vegetables, fruits...
Well, the modern farming is going to destroy our environment but our people as well. The pesticide is not just poison for the small animals and insect, it is poison for the people. But, on the other hand, we are 8 billion people and  without GMO food, herbicide and pesticide we will be died very soon. So, the organic food is just in theory. For example, if you going to eat organic food which is so healthy for us,  you will spend a lot of time and money. If you are going to keep organic food in your store, it will be last just three or four days. If you put in the store, for example potatoes in the fridge it will be last 3 or four months.  Old methods about farming we must forget. Huge farming and slaughterhouses will be with us for the centuries.     
I care about food produced but what can I do? If they offer me poison, I must buy poison, because I do not have any other choice. just go to the shop and get it or leave it!

Cheers
Ivan

text: This video offers a concise and thorough breakdown of the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development and the SDGs. It provides a clear overview of the agenda’s universal adoption in 2015, tracing its roots from Agenda 21 and the Millennium Development Goals. The focus on the 5Ps of sustainability (People, Planet, Prosperity, Peace, and Partnership) is well-explained, along with a detailed look at the 17 SDGs and their guiding principles—Integration, Universality, Inclusivity, and "Leave No One Behind" (LNOB). This video is an excellent resource for anyone looking to quickly grasp the core elements of the 2030 Agenda and the Sustainable Development Goals.

text:
Before people start blaming people as a whole, realize that humans have existed on the Earth for thousands of years without significantly impacting the environment until the past two centuries on the onset of mass industrialization and agriculture. So do not say humans are a cancer that do not deserve the Earth, but the problem is how we are living the modern life.

Human society is living with the highest standards in history, but the modern life comes with costs. Consumerism with one use products such as wrappers and boxes for the products some people take granted for creates a lot of trash. The electricity we use for all our mechanical processes are mostly created by burning fossil fuels (Renewable Energy such as solar can only do so much. . .). The Cars and computer that you use to live your modern life were created by factories that emit greenhouse gases and require the mining of resources that shift the surface.

Here is the hard truth. Consumerism and the modern life is a great life, but that comes with clear costs. If you truly want to save the Earth, then understand that the human race as a whole must sacrifice many of its wants and desires and basically live with *less* in order to leave a smaller environmental impact. Until people figure out a way to somehow collect and recycle all the waste created by constant buying, spending, and throwing away of our trash, this is the only realistic option if we want to protect the planet in the long run.

text:
One evening, while living in the Rio Grande Valley and helping my father cover his citrus trees to help protect them against a freeze predicted for later that night, I found myself hoping and praying for heavy overcast to replace the current clear skies. We’d never heard of the greenhouse effect at that time, but we knew that a blanket of clouds (water vapor) would slow down the escape of Earth’s heat.

My dad had already flooded the ground around the trees and prepped his smudge pots to be lit at the appropriate moment (for the blanketing effect of carbon laden air).

As the thermometer continued to slowly but surely drop, we nervously sat listening to the reports on the radio. Then, just as temperatures neared freezing, a bank of clouds moved in over the region and the rate of temperature decrease slowed and stopped at 30 degrees (F.). An hour later, the sun peeked over the horizon and very slowly the temperature began to rise. Damage to the fruit and trees was avoided without having lit the smudge pots.

My point is that even though some people may not believe in the greenhouse effect, they’re almost certainly not citrus growers.

text: Scientists and the IPCC have known for years that CO2 has three climate change properties: one that warms (the greenhouse gas effect) and two that cool (the flora and aerosol effects). While there have been dozens of studies on the greenhouse gas effect, there have only been five on the total effect of CO2. All five concluded the same thing. CO2 cools more than it warms. The last study that included 62+ years of real-time records and hourly and monthly datasets from every international climate institute (NASA, NOAA, Berkeley, Hadley, York, World Meteorological Organization (Tokyo), RSS, UAH, and Mauna Loa Observatory found that the net effects of water vapor and methane force climate change. But, the total effect of CO2 (greenhouse, flora, and aerosol effects combined) is to anti-force climate. I really feel sorry for all the organizations that discounted flora and aerosol effects and the educators that now have to admit that they don't always teach what is real.

text:
ChatGPT read the text for this video and responded to my query of its veracity (no wonder the fact-checkers are out to get you, Dennis): 

This PragerU video presents a flawed argument that minimizes the threat of climate change and ignores the overwhelming scientific consensus that human activity is causing global warming.

Firstly, the video's claim that climate change policies have led to a shortage of fossil fuels is misleading. The decrease in the use of fossil fuels is a response to the need to mitigate climate change and transition to sustainable energy sources, rather than a policy that directly leads to a shortage of fossil fuels. Additionally, the video ignores the fact that renewable energy sources, such as solar and wind power, have seen a rapid increase in usage and are becoming increasingly competitive with fossil fuels.

Furthermore, the video's argument that fossil fuel shortages have led to inflation, job losses, and mass starvation is also questionable. While higher energy prices can have an impact on certain industries, it is not the sole cause of inflation or job losses. The video also fails to acknowledge that the impacts of climate change, such as more frequent and intense natural disasters, can have significant economic consequences.

The video's assertion that using fossil fuels is necessary to protect against extreme weather events and alleviate drought is unsupported by evidence. The use of fossil fuels is actually one of the main causes of climate change, which increases the frequency and severity of extreme weather events and exacerbates drought conditions.
Finally, the video's claim that nuclear energy is the most promising alternative to fossil fuels is also debatable. While nuclear energy does not produce greenhouse gas emissions, it has its own set of safety and waste disposal challenges, and the cost of building and operating nuclear power plants is often prohibitively high.

In conclusion, the PragerU video presents a one-sided and misleading argument that downplays the risks of climate change and advocates for continued fossil fuel use. The scientific evidence overwhelmingly supports the need for a transition to renewable energy sources to mitigate the impacts of climate change and ensure a sustainable future for generations to come.

text:
As the climate heads into greater extremes globally, how can we best deal with future climate crises? 
The short answer is that we cannot deal with them unless we take care of nature's inner balance.
We live in a tightly-closed and interdependent system in which everything boomerangs back to us. While living in such a system, we need to reconsider what we want and think, and how we treat each other, because our human connections are the primary influence on how nature responds to us.
It is common to think that climate is dependent on factors outside of us—whether it be balances between heat and cold in the environment, or the effects of various kinds of pollution we emit—because we lack a complete picture of how our attitudes to each other bring about the strongest responses from nature toward us.
No creature distorts nature the way that we people do. And it is not simply a matter of switching to renewable energy sources, electric cars and the like; it is a matter of how we relate to each other.
If we truly wish to witness more balance throughout nature and not have to deal with all kinds of cold waves and other natural disasters, then similarly to how we have electricity, water and gas meters in our homes, we should also have meters that count how much evil we emit into the world from our negative attitudes to each other. What I mean is that if we could feel the extent to which we emit negative forces into the world, which negatively ricochet back to us, then we would wish to change this negative driver within us. We would want to switch it to a drive that makes our human connections positive, and which harmonizes us with nature.
In simple terms, when we get up in the morning, we should first and foremost consider what we need to do in order for all people to have it good. Developing such an attitude is not so simple, yet we will need to seriously work on it as we head into the future. A life of increasing blows from nature or a life of peace and harmony depends on the extent to which we impact a shift in our attitudes to each other—from negative to positive.

text:
3 years ago I graduated with my master's in economics. The economic viability of a carbon free energy grid was part of my main topic (whether there was an economic long term case for nuclear power in a green energy grid was the main topic.) 
At the time the  general consensus was that it was physically possible to make a carbon free electric grid but that it wasn't economically viable in any way. At least not right now and that building more nuclear would make it easier when prices of batteries in particular would fall in a decade or so. In p[articular Nuclear could function as a base load that reduces the reliance on battery capacity in the few weeks where it is neither sunny or windy.

Now 3 years later and prices for large batteries are a quarter of what they were were when I graduated. It is honestly insane how fast a lot of it has progressed especially in the 100 kWh to 2 MWh range.

And now perovskite solar panels seem like they will actually become possible within the next decade, promising a massive increase in the possible energy production. The new panels by oxford PV are very promising for the future. 

There is much to be optimistic about.

text:
A couple things that werent mentioned that i think are interesting:
- population growth is slowing down drastically. Many developped countries now have less children than needed to keep the population the same, and even less developed countries have less children than before. Less people = less consumption
- theres lots of renewable energy projects in africa and other third world countries, where they dont have to replace the old systems and are therefore easier to impliment. Its easier to do it right from the start!
- theres lots of interesting scientific developments happening such as a guy that figured out a way to turn plastic into petrol (using only solar),  plastic eating bacteria, hydro motors, etc.
- theres more and more projects focussed not just on quantity but quality of farming and reforestation, with more focus on native species, polycultures, keystone species, etc.

Some ongoing issues that i havent heard you mentioned
- our ocean biomes. The biggest carbon sink in the world is still our ocean, where algea does a lot of the heavy lifting. And we are still very very rapidly destroying our oceans with deep through fishing, overfishing, dumping lots of trash in the ocean etc.
- meat and agricultural industries: arent slowing down much. Lots of small projects are trying to problem solve by reducing waste, and finding vegetarian alterntives that are often problematic in their own way.
- lithium batteries: lithium is just as sustainable as fossil fuels: if you gotta dig it up, its not sustainable. And with electrification were basically just shifting the focus, from fosil fuels being the problem, to lithium, instead of solving the real problem which is overconsumption.
- same goes for uranium, with the added issue of radioactivity.
- the ecological feedbackloops that have been set into action such as the icecaps melting, adding more carbon in the air, increasing the melt. (Also look into AMOC) Or the rainforests no longer sustaining their own climate as they dont evaporate enough anymore, etc. 

Id love if anyone has imput in any if hese points, or has more!

text:
Such a money-eating government and judiciary—truly heartbreaking to witness. It feels like chips off the same old block.

Aur sabse zyada dukh tab hota hai jab jo log kabhi is topic par padhai ya samajh hi nahi rakhte, wahi log judgment dene lagte hain. Unhe kya pata hota hai ki reservation ka misuse karke job lena, judiciary ya politics mein pahunchna kya consequences laata hai?

Aaj desh ka haal chahe politically dekho ya environmentally—dono hi tarah se kaafi kharab ho chuka hai. Aur ab toh situation yeh ho gayi hai ki agar aap sarkar ke rules ya Supreme Court ke judgments par sawal uthao, ya unhe bekaar ya galat bolo, toh turant aap par desh-virodhi ya desh-drohi ka tag laga diya jaata hai.

Sach mein sharam aani chahiye humein—jis desh mein prakriti ki pooja ki jaati hai, usi desh mein pollution aaj all-time high par hai. Aur jin deshon mein nature ko bas “nature” maana jaata hai, wahan rivers, mountains, forests sab zyada behtar condition mein hain.

Yeh sirf irony nahi, balki hamari collective failure ka proof hai.

text:
I teach social studies. We talk a lot about the impact of race, ethnicity, gender, and class on the development of the modern world, and how policies developed decades or centuries ago reverberate into today. I _do_ show them how those policies have been used, intentionally or accidentally, to give advantage or disadvantage to groups of people, because it's manifestly true that this occurs. I'm not afraid to use the phrase white privilege in that discussion, because I can see that I have advantages in America as a White man that a Black person doesn't benefit from. However...
I also always say something like this to my (mostly White) students, "None of what we are talking is a reflection of you or your character. None of this history is your fault, and it's not your parent's fault, either. I don't want you to feel personally guilty if you're White, and if you're Black, Latino, Asian, or Native, this doesn't mean that you or any of your ancestors were necessarily passive victims with no voice. We are all inheritors of the systems that were developed over the course of history, and we have to understand how those choices have impacted the world we live in. However, asking that question doesn't mean that you are personally being blamed, and I don't want _any_ of you to feel that you are."
*Edit* To be clear, in response to an accusation below, I'm  not saying that my approach is unusual or unique insightful. I think it's how many of my colleagues approach the issue. I suspect, though, that many fed on a diet of Fox News would still call this Critical Race Theory, which I think is a much more nuanced and complicated concept than would be taught in a secondary-level survey course. If anyone has a problem with teaching the idea that racism and ethnic prejudice influenced our institutions and continues to shape our society today, then they're asking us to teach a blinkered and inaccurate version of American history. I wonder, though, what Rep. Crenshaw would say.

I don't understand why nuclear wasn't discussed within the climate change conversation. It's always a he-who-shall-not-be-named thing because of its inherent risk (for liberals) and overhead costs (for conservatives). Yet in the last 6 months ALONE we have made leaps at moon-landing levels of progress for nuclear, specifically *fusion nuclear power*. Nuclear fusion carries no risk of radiation, and is highly efficient once fully harnessed. This would be a win-win for Democrats and Republicans alike (low risk of danger, and highly cost efficient). 

The nuanced conversation would then be about how to fund it. So WHY are we shying away from that conversation when it's the perfect middle ground for both sides to see eye to eye on as a solution forward?

text:
White student: "Mom, am I evil because I am white?"

I can understand that this would be a very unsettling encounter for any parent. However, I can't help but wonder how many Black students held (and continue to harbor) negative interpretations of self because of how our ancestors have been written into history. I can't help but wonder how many of these students might have grown up with an under-developed sense of personal value when history is being taught with no nuance and compassion.

As controversial as CRT is, I can appreciate the approach it takes as far as describing how America's institutions were formed and maintained over the years. And while it might be easy to sit in the 21st century and deny the significant role that race played so many decades ago, let us not forget that in the earliest days of America's development, Black people were literally seen and valued as cargo. We were non-human. On a balance sheet slaves would have been represented among the plantation's fixed assets, like livestock and premises. 

This classification is not insignificant, because it was the unpaid labor provided by these slaves that was the basis of agriculture in the US. Out of that success came the proliferation of several ancillary services, including shipping, insurance, banking and others. To support and protect the plantation economy, police militias and the court system were developed, etc.

text:
Two points I’d like to make: 

First: We aren’t “Waiting for China”. Advancing to an unreliable energy source too soon is a security issue. Joe Biden said just last year that President Xi personally told him that he will “own the U.S. in 15 years”. Energy is a security issue and that should be part of the discussion as well. 

Secondly, saying the “border is wide open!” Has no bearing on wether or not it’s actually is. Example: people can say “the border is wide open”, that can encourage more people to flood the border. But, if the border is secure, the illegals crossings won’t happen. Regardless of the “talking points”, the border is either secure, or it is not. Today, it is not imo. 

I appreciate this conversation but I believe it is the comedian in Trevor Noah here that is trying to find the humor in a topic or simply downplay the serious of the event for comedic purposes.

text:
These docs are so frustrating. They're like advertising with no critical thinking. A few points...
1- There never was in history, and still isn't any energy transition. It's only addition.
2- The "circular economy" that works "only" on solar, wind and biofuel ignores all the diesel trucks moving things around.
3- Oslo is the capital of an oil producing country that still imports emissions from abroad. From memory, only the USA does that (maybe poor countries too). It's complete hypocrisy. 
4- EFFICIENCY INCREASES ENERGY DEMAND. We've known this for 150 years, yet we continue to bank on this. We're making policies based on a cognitive biases.
5- Replacing wood with hydrogen is not more sustainable. It may make life easier for those poor people but it's not a climate mitigation measure. BTW, we still get twice as much energy from wood than solar and wind. Rocket mass heaters are great for heating with little wood.

text:
Doing more with less is great, but it's not sustainable. Slowing down our total emissions will be crucial, but it will not be enough if the global populations keeps increasing at an exponential rate.
As stated in the video, more people leads to an increased demand for resources. An increased demand for resources leads to more emissions. Economists today often forget the fact that there is no such thing as an ever growing economy. That fact is probably the biggest problem with measuring an economy in terms of GDP. To fix both the climate crisis and the world financial crisis, we are going to have to do two major things. First, re-define what a successful economy looks like, and secondly, we have to implement some sort of population control.
The most reasonable form of population control would be a 2-child policy. (Yes, I'm aware that statistically speaking, you would need 2,1 children for every family. But we have to get the global population shrinking first.)

text:
What most people seem to overlook when it comes to renewables 
1 - Is the massive amount of damage being done to the earth to acquire the minerals needed for them. 

2 - The very real problem that there probably isn’t enough copper on earth to meet the demand of renewables. 

3 - The absolute unreliability of renewables. The wind doesn’t always blow and the sun doesn’t always shine even during the daytime. 

4 - The vast amount of land that would need to be covered with solar panels near the areas the power is needed just doesn’t exist. 

5 - Every single renewable generation source, i.e. solar panels and wind turbines, must be replaced on average every 12-15 years. 

That means the entire cost will recur every 15 years forever!! And most of those things can’t be recycled.

text:
In order for certain new technology to be expressed, or to be released, there has to be a willingness to receive it. And this requires both the change in the collective consciousness but it also requires that there are certain scientists who are willing to question what most scientists are currently not questioning, including materialism.
Now, just as a concrete example here, you have the whole concept of free energy. This can be explained actually in terms of what is currently known by quantum physics. You have observations made by quantum physicists in bubble chambers, that you can have a state where there are no particles, suddenly one particle appears, divides itself into several particles, they collide again and then disappear. There have been some scientists who have been willing to speculate that, beyond what can be observed even at the subatomic level, there must be some kind of energy field, some call it a quantum field, some call it a ground state, whatever they call it, but it has basically been proven by quantum physics, that there is some state beyond the physical material realm. And in that state there is energy. And that energy can then enter the material realm and become physical matter. If you take this and put it together with a big bang, you can see that, is it actually logical that all of the matter and all of the energy that is now in this huge universe was compressed into something called a singularity? Whatever that may be. Is this really logical?
And this means that all of the matter that you see in the world today is actually created from energy. This was proven by Einstein. And that energy must have come from somewhere, and what has now been proven for those who are willing to see it by quantum physics, is that that energy came from another realm. It entered the material world, the physical realm from another realm. Well, is that not free energy? Is it not thereby proven that the entire world is created by free energy? So does that not mean it is also possible that technology could be created, that would be able to, so to speak, channel the energy from a different realm into the material realm, where it could then be used to perform physical work or at least create electricity that could power machines that perform physical work.

text: I started my degree in Environmental Science in 2011, hoping to do some good. I went to grad school, worked in NGOS and research institutes, etc. So far, I've only seen things  get worse and worse. The end-of-the-century worst case scenarios that I learned in school are happening not only faster, but worse than expected (as Rockstrom shows). This year I actually walked away from the field and switched to a regular corporate job, as far away from sustainability as possible. After all I've seen and done, I am 100% confident if WWIII doesn't kill us, climate change will. Both are just around the corner. We truly are a break in evolution :(

What a shame that big Corp didn't hear when the problem was announced back in the 50's, yes the 50s! Charles David Keeling started measuring CO2 levels at Mauna Loa Observatory in Hawaii, creating the "Keeling Curve," which showed a clear upward trend in CO2 concentrations in the atmosphere. This was one of the first pieces of evidence that human activities affected the global climate.
The sad thing is that they're still not hearing it. The only sound they hear is the sound of money.

text:
I’m a horticulturist in Rome, GA. In springs of 2021 and 22 days were 75F but two nights got up to 80F in town and ALL vegetable crops stopped growing. They did not die they just stopped in place and immature fruits rotted on the plants.
Look up Plant DIF for mechanism. Old Hort technique to control greenhouse crops timing and height by decreasing the day/night temp difference but no one reversed it ever…this was before hormones were used.
But this doesn’t happen …not in nature…not known. Not expected or predicted or in any models.
When a single night is warmer than the days vegetables will stop growing in the affected areas.
My name is Jimmy Greer and I stand by my observations

text:
I am not optimistic that our species is willing to take the required actions.  Firstly, 'we' (the collective we) do not actually view ourselves as part of any collective.  Secondly, too many individuals can't (or won't) even take actions (for just themselves) on a daily/monthly/yearly basis that they KNOW is necessary to have a future (addictions, inertia (laziness), and the high probability that they've already exhausted the resources ($) needed to yield better future for themselves or their offspring (education/learning a trade/etc.).    

We are not a 'wise' species, yes we are clever, but at an individual level and not at a holistic level.  While we have some individuals who are wise and can see the writing on the wall, their numbers are too few to bring the rest (kicking and screaming all the way) along to do what is necessary.   

The Earth has gone through massive changes in its surface biological content many times (most of which absent of all but the most simplistic forms of life) and the Earth will proceed without us just the same.  I hate any use of the phrase that we must 'Save the Planet' since 'The Planet' doesn't need saving, it is a very narrow set of environmental conditions that must be saved if Humans are to remain a viable species...but the universe (or even this tiny spec of such) won't care if this particular example of a pseudo-self aware biological entity overruns its petri dish and knew it was overrunning it near the end.  Sad? to whom? and to those who would invoke a deity that 'cares' then I would speculate that if such existed it might simply shrug its shoulders and make a note to the effect 'ah,heck, another failure' before trying again.

3 options
You are right:
1. Climate change is unrelated to humans and we change nothing. We enjoy a few more years of our familiar lives until climate change eventually kills most humans. 

You are wrong:
1. Climate change is related to humans and we change nothing. We enjoy a few more years of our familiar lives until climate change eventually kills most humans. 
2. Climate change is related to humans and we make the changes scientist have suggested. Climate change is potentially reversed and humans/animals survive.

text: Most people seem to miss an important point in these discussions. As noted in this video, the rate and amplitude of warming is accelerating. Atmospheric GHG levels continue to increase and there is no way to rapidly remove them from the atmosphere, that will require centuries. When you factor in further feedbacks from loss of albedo due to melting snow and ice, loss of the aerosol masking effect, and rising methane levels from the warming soils then we are looking at +5 degrees of warming within several decades. The heat will kill us long before sea rise becomes catastrophic. Intelligent solar radiation management strategies are imperitive if we want to live long enough to solve the carbon problem.
video_title: The Tipping Points of Climate Change — and Where We Stand | Johan Rockström | TED

text: The problem that nobody ever discusses is that responding to climate change is comparable to joining a religion. NOT because the facts are a matter of faith. They are hard facts! However, taking the necessary steps to address those facts is not the  coldly rational choice that people like Mr. Rockstrom present. It requires faith, deep dedication, and a willingness to suffer profoundly for an ideal. Even something as trivial as "Fast Fashion" is deeply entwined into the psyche and self image of the people socially invested in it. You do not just say "that drug that you are addicted to is killing you. Just stop!" You don't say, "Your marriage is toxic and dangerous. Just leave!" We realize that these are situations that are deeply complex on multiple levels and anything but simple rational choices to make. We have multitudes of sup[port networks, psychological and chemical treatments, established medical/emotional protocols for leading people out of danger. The unsustainable lives that we are living are our reality. To save the planet, ALL of us must be willing to endure deep emotional pain and personal sacrifice for what is still a largely abstract principle, however certain the outcome. If there is any doubt, just look at the climate change advocates. I would be very surprised if Mr. Rockstrom arrived for his talk on a bicycle. That is not a criticism of him. It simply underlines the fact that we are not willing/able to do more than talk and whole heartedly agree with those who talk. How many of the people applauding are vegans who drive electric vehichles? How many people commenting here are? We must start figuring out actionable functional plans to help people through withdrawal from their addiction to living.

text:
Our planetary pickle is heart-breaking.  Planetary boundaries is a useful framework... but surprising that Roskstrom fails to put all the pieces together.  Climate change (along with all the other planetary boundaries) are symptom predicaments of the fundamental predicament which is our ECOLOGICAL OVERSHOOT.  Narrow focus on counting carbon in a futile attempt to address climate change is leading us to worsen overshoot.  Attempting to continue powering this civilisation with any energy source will accelerate global biosphere collapse.  Our human systems are in collapse because the earth systems we depend on are in collapse.  We can only make things less bad by powering down this self-terminating techno-industrial civilisation.  He's right about strapping in... it's not going to be a pretty.  No solutions... just responses... build local community resilience.  As the system breaks down and becomes overwhelmed by cascading and worsening crises, the only help available will be from your neighbours and local community.  
If you haven't heard of ecological overshoot, learn about it and try to inform those around you.  Our chance of mitigating the unimaginable suffering that is unfolding is dependent on as many people understanding this concept.  William Catton's 'OVERSHOOT' should be mandatory reading for all humans who care about the community of life on this beautiful blue-green world.

text: I work in the residential housing and construction industry. We have an impending update to the building code which uses new "green" technologies as a baseline for construction projects, including things like structural supports in the roof for solar panels, plugs in the garage for EVs and battery storage, heat pumps for winter heating and summer cooling, efficient air exchangers, increased thermal insulation ratings, better window and door construction, and passive heating and cooling options for new builds. All new homes will be meeting these standards, however, it will be at consumer cost, and more importantly, will not extend retroactively to the existing housing stock or commercial buildings due for refits. We need government tax breaks and incentives to encourage homebuyers to make these switches for themselves, to go from natural gas or propane heating to heat pumps, and from energy intensive appliances and vehicles to low-carbon options like induction ovens, low-flow toilets, and EVs. Selling this as cost-saving measures for an average household needs to be more enticing to offset the up-front costs of adoption in order to prepare people for heat waves and potential brown outs. We need these technologies implemented at scale in rental housing units as well, without "renovicting" thousands and pushing the cost of a single apartment dwelling out of reach for the average renter. And we are still caught in the snare of inaction when it comes to proposals for housing being built in accessible, public-transit friendly areas, as opposed to using up more land for suburban sprawl. If you want better action when it comes to urban planning, better transit, less traffic congestion, lower utility bills, and affordable housing, start pressuring your local city councilors and mayors to re-evaluate their current land and housing stock and business portfolios. There is ample room for smart infill that would put less stress on infrastructure systems, using less natural greenspace for development, and that offer residents comfortable living environments that won't be overcrowded, physically or socially disconnected from the rest of the town/city, and that are far more economically and environmentally friendly. As the speaker said, the time for action was yesterday, and we don't have a lot of time to waste in any sector in order for our species and the millions of other creatures on this planet to have a viable future. Things are still set to get worse before they get better, and we must prepare ourselves for the ever-rising temperatures in our near future.

text:
In the shadows of science, the Earth cries out,
A plea from the depths, where we’ve lost our doubt.
Years of alarms, now sounding the horn,
Our planet reshaped, our future forlorn.

Earth system scientists, their hands in the air,
Nervous and wary, a burden they bear.
For we’ve underestimated the speed of the storm,
Now witnessing changes that defy the norm.

We’ve breached the bounds, the planetary frame,
Where resilience falters, and we bear the shame.
Abrupt changes unfold, faster than thought,
In a world where stability is painfully sought.

The Paris accords, a decade gone by,
Yet the temperature climbs, as we reach for the sky.
1.2 degrees, a blistering rise,
A record heat that blinds our eyes.

But what’s worse is the pace, a terrifying trend,
Warming accelerates, where will it end?
If we keep on this path, we'll break through the veil,
To 2 degrees, where life begins to pale.

Beyond carbon alone, every element aches,
From freshwater drained to ecosystems’ breaks.
The sixth mass extinction whispers in the night,
As nature’s balance tips, lost in our sight.

Economic costs mount, human lives at stake,
In the heat, in the floods, as the Earth starts to quake.
$38 trillion lost by 2050’s dawn,
If we don't change course, the future is gone.

The ocean, once a buffer, now sounds the alarm,
Its warmth rises sharply, a sign of great harm.
In 2023, it veered off the chart,
A symptom of imbalance tearing apart.

Land loses its grip, no longer a sink,
The Amazon shifts, as the world starts to shrink.
The forests, the reefs, the ice sheets we mourn,
As tipping points near, and new climates are born.

The great acceleration, a tale of our time,
Where growth meets destruction, and nature’s fine line.
Scientists are shaking, their voices grow hoarse,
As they warn us to steer back on the course.

What will it take for us to see,
The perilous path that leads to the sea,
Where oceans rise and forests fall,
And humanity's future is a fragile call?

We must bend the curve, cut the emissions,
Navigate wisely through perilous conditions.
For five short years, the carbon clock ticks,
Before our planet faces irreversible tricks.

The path is clear, the science is strong,
We must act now, or we’ll face the wrong.
For the Earth is our home, our one true place,
And we cannot afford to lose this race.

In the hands of the few, the choice is laid,
To protect our world or let it fade.
And as the scientists urge, with nerves frayed and worn,
Let us heed their call, before the storm.

text: A potentially fortunate factor that we see on every single planet and moon that has any atmosphere, is that temperature can be closely predicted via only two variables - air pressure and distance from the sun.  Whether it is Venus or Titan, if you go to an altitude where air pressure is equal to earth the ONLY variable that is needed to accurately predict temperature is distance from the sun.  High up in the atmosphere of Venus, where air pressure equals earth, temperature is ONLY 1 degree C higher than earth (normalized for it being closer to the sun), even though Venus is 750,000 parts per million carbon dioxide, and earth is currently 400 parts per million.  How is it possible that we are seeing temperatures rising on earth as carbon dioxide rises, while Venus is the same temperature as Earth with almost 2,000 times more carbon dioxide in its atmosphere? It is difficult to be sure.  One theory is that, at a certain concentration of carbon dioxide, there is enough in the atmosphere that it essentially captures all of the heat from the light spectrum that it blocks.  After that, at any higher concentration of carbon dioxide, planets cease to show a rise in temperatures.  On earth, that level of carbon dioxide is predicted to be about 500 parts per million.  If that is correct, then we can only have maybe a 1/2 to 1 degree additional increase in temperature from increasing carbon dioxide, and after that temperature will no longer be impacted by higher levels in the atmosphere.  That would explain why in the earth's distant history, carbon dioxide was ten times higher than it is now, but the temperature was basically the same as the temperature today.  It does make you wonder why some people like the presenter here is sure that a run away global heating event is possible on earth, when all facts we see on other planets and in earth's own history refute that notion.  I am not a climate denier.  I think it is incredibly stupid for us to be artificially rapidly increasing the carbon level in our atmosphere.  But I am willing to look at facts that are present without blindly believing in unproven theories that are alarmist and inconsistent with what we observe.

The average temperature of the Earth at the surface is 14.5 °C, but without any greenhouse gases, it would be around -18°C.
Therefore greenhouse gases are important for our survival.
Water vapour is one of the key greenhouse gases. There is more water vapour than any other greenhouse gas in the atmosphere.
The increase in man-made greenhouse gases is the primary driver of current climate change.   
Water vapor acts as a feedback mechanism, amplifying warming caused by man-made gases.
Man made greenhouse gases increase the temperature which increases the water vapour which increases the temperature apparently...
But it is so complicated and man made gases may not be that important given that WATER VAPOUR is the key component and there is a feedback component. 
The amount of water in the seas overwhelms everything else.
A global average humidity is difficult to pinpoint, but it's generally considered to be around 70-80% relative humidity. 
Humidity can range from near zero in very dry deserts to close to 100% in humid tropical regions.

Given all that, is man made climate change real?

The worst part of climate change is that the first people to be hurt will be those with the smallest margins and minimal safety nets, people like subsistence farmers and reef fishermen. 

Meanwhile, people in the U.S. will still be complaining about "high" gas prices (that are still lower than what most of the world pays), emissions regulations, "unsightly" wind turbines and solar farms, and refuse to participate in international agreements in the name of national sovereignty.

And before someone tries to claim that China and India are bigger problems, India consumes less than half the energy the U.S. consumes, despite having more than four times the population. China does consume more energy than the U.S., but the U.S.'s per capita consumption is more than 2.5 times higher; most of China's energy goes to the industrial sector, while less than one-third of U.S. energy goes to industry, and the U.S devotes more one-quarter of its energy to transportation, which is a small sliver of China's energy usage. Yes, China and India have serious environmental issues to contend with, but the U.S.'s contribution to emissions is very disproportionate to its population, so it has a disproportionate responsibility to combat climate change.

text:
A couple years ago it reached 40 degrees in Canada on Vancouver island. This may not sound like a lot to hotter countries but to the people and animals here it was devastating. Fish floated dead in lakes, people left water out for birds and wildlife which struggled not to die on the side of the road from the heat wave. It was bad for the people too, who are accustomed to  ranges of -10 to 20 degrees Celsius. A significant portion of our seniors could not cope with the sudden change in temperature and died. Hospitals were overrun by the younger generation which did not die but still suffered heat strokes due to not everyone being able to afford a/c and biologically not being climatized to such high temperatures. (We are talking about people who go around in shorts in 5 degree weather, they’re used to producing and retaining heat, not dispersing it.)
It was a record breaking temperature for the area. It could have been a freak incident but if we continue as we are I’m sure eventually it will be normal and we will lose a large fraction of our wildlife. If the change happens to quickly we may continue to see people die because they simply can’t cope with a temperature flux that far out of their bodies normal.

text:
I'm 80 years old, born and grew up in Holland, emigrated to Canada ( Saskatchewan) in 1973, I believe climate change is real, experienced it myself, in Europe as well as in Canada. 

 As long there have been humans on this earth we have been burning wood and later on fossil  fuels, coal, oil etc. None of this ever changed the climate , not even during the industrial revolution in the 1800's 

The changes in weather patterns did not start happening until the 1970's, why you ask me ? 
After WWII the world population was approx. 2.5 billion people, this has grown to approx. 8 billion and still growing.
We all contribute to CO2 pollution, everything we own has been made in factories that burn fossil fuels of some sort.
Most of us drive cars that produce CO2, but all this pollution is more ore less at ground level.

In my opinion the biggest pollution that affects our climate is the enormous explosion of airline traffic, look at the contrails in the sky, all CO2 and water vapor, 10 to 12 kilometers high trapping the heat at lower levels.
The amount of fuel these jets burn is enormous, a 737 takes on about 20 TONNES, a 747 close to 200 TONNES, all that is burnt in the upper atmosphere.

And everything comes by plane nowadays, not just the millions of people that fly but the amount of cargo is immense.

Something else that is never talked about is the massive amount of oxygen that is required to burn all that jet fuel, so we take the oxygen out of the upper atmosphere, which is very "thin" already  and we leave CO2 and water and a bunch of other pollutants up there.
How good is that for our weather.

And of course all these planes completely disturb the natural air currents that take place up there. But according to our governments that only contributes only 6% to climate change, I think they forgot to put a zero behind that 6.

So, this is only the jets, what about all the rockets we shoot into space for space exploration and to bring into orbit the thousands of satellites that circle around the earth, they leave a massive amount of pollution in the stratosphere and damage the ozone layer that protects us from solar radiation.

This is my explanation of why climate change has only been happening since the last 50 or 60 years.
I invite anyone to comment.

text: Great video.  Except the prediction dates are wrong. Most of the things mentioned are here NOW. A few years ago, there was debate among those experts who didn't want to scare people and those who saw that what was being said was incorrect. In fact, since the 1.5 Paris target...selected because of the harms experts thought might happen at that level...every IPCC the harms per tenth of a degree have been revised. We now know that 1.0 should have been the target. We're basically at 1.5 now. And the feedbacks mean there is acceleration (I can explain to anyone interested). We'll hit 2.0 almost no matter what we do before 2050. And the harms will be more on par with what we thought 3.0 might be. Much of the damage is baked in due to feedbacks that will continue to melt ice. So our actions now, if EXTREME, will stabilize at least near 3.0. Yes, every tenth of a degree matters but the way that narrative is used lacks the necessary urgency about what the predicament truly looks like. I'm personally watching the majority out there ignore this, most will not be ready for what is happening by 2050. I'm losing my "collective good" ethos for an "every person for themselves" mentality to go where I can with intentional communities who align. Smart folks will come along. Sorry Shannon, this was a pretty presentation.

text: I spent half of my career working at universities, and Dr. Curry is 100% correct. Unbeknownst to most people, university research professors have to fund themselves, that means going out and getting grants and contracts before they get to do their research. They also need to crank out papers in peer journals to establish their credibility, they usually manage a team of grad students, usually they have to teach at least one class per semester, conferences, network with colleagues, etc.  That's a lot to do, and they have to constantly be aware to continue funding. The easiest path is to the low hanging fruit, where is most of the grant money? Easy to find when the government is soliciting for certain research, so they give the customer what they want, if you want me to prove there's global warming I'll prove it, if you want me to prove the next ice age I'll prove that too.

text: Tony Heller's channel is a good source for debunking the climate crisis lies.  I also recommend reading Dr. Tim Ball's book The Deliberate Corruption of Climate Science.  I also like to track my local temperatures to see if anything the alarmist's say makes sense.  The UN Secretary-General said that the planet is boiling, but here in Springfield, Illinois we've had a mild summer.  We were below our historical average temperature in both June and July.  That hasn't happened since 2013.  I've been tracking our temperatures since 2007.  Our historical average annual temperature is 54 degrees F.  Here are our temperatures in order since 2007: 55, 52, 53, 54, 55, 57, 52, 51, 55, 57, 57, 54, 53, 54, 55, and 53 degree F.  The climate alarmists claim that rising levels of carbon dioxide cause the temperature to increase.  Since carbon dioxide levels have increased every year, we should see increasing temperatures every year if their theory is correct.  Instead, you see that temperatures fluctuate both up and down from year to year.  There is no statistically significant correlation between carbon dioxide levels and temperature.

I didn't understand how pervasive the cult-like dogma was until I started teaching an Earth Science course over which I have very little control of the content and presentation.  The course textbook and supporting materials are one-sided, leaning heavily on the "overwhelming scientific consensus" meme and the students arrive pre-programmed to believe that what they've been fed by the schools and our culture is their irrefutable gospel.  I've tried to guide them toward asking questions and at least considering that what they've been taught is not necessarily factually correct.  It's ironic that "climate science" has devolved to the point where science is no longer part of the picture.  Testing hypotheses, analyzing actual data, and drawing conclusions based on valid, repeatable results is not a process of which the students are aware. text: My uncle’s sister in-law did a PhD in the early ‘70’s on the likely reduced yields from grain production in Canada. She had funding from fertiliser manufactures keen to stress the importance of maintaining yields as the Earth cooled. During the 1970s getting funding required buying into the global cooling hysteria. Then as now more rational minds were silenced or mocked for saying it was just natural variation. The British Sunday Times Magazine did an article, were every one lived at the equator, and most of Europe, USA, Canada were under ice. The same magazine, sometime around 2004 (ish) did a similar article, only this time every one was now going to be living at the North Pole, with most of Europe, USA, Canada either too warm or under water. Our family have farmed at 54 degrees North since 1724, with , very detailed records, especially after mid 1850s soil, air temperature records are accurate and detailed, arable yields are also detailed very accurately. As of 2023, air, soil temperatures are unchanged, yields weighted for improved productivity ( in terms of mechanical technology change, improved varieties, improved pest control etc) are as one would expect. While  temperature, rain fall, frosts etc have remained on average constant, the tropospheric carbon dioxide percentage has shown an increase which has translated into a longer growing season, better frost tolerance, more robust arid period tolerance, greater pest tolerance and over all increased yield per acreage, with less fertiliser input.

For decades, the country’s leading oil and gas companies have understood the science of climate change and the dangers posed by fossil fuels. Year after year, top executives heard it from their own scientists whose warnings were explicit and often dire.

In 1979, an Exxon study said that burning fossil fuels “will cause dramatic environmental effects” in the coming decades.

“The potential problem is great and urgent,” it concluded.

But instead of heeding the evidence of the research they were funding, major oil firms worked together to bury the findings and manufacture a counter narrative to undermine the growing scientific consensus around climate science. The fossil fuel industry’s campaign to create uncertainty paid off for decades by muddying public understanding of the growing dangers from global heating and stalling political action.

text: This  explains that climate change refers to long-term shifts in weather patterns, while global warming is a persistent rise in Earth's temperature caused primarily by increased greenhouse gases. The largest driver of these emissions is the burning of fossil fuels for industrial activity and transportation. The impacts are widespread, including melting polar ice, retreating glaciers, rising sea levels that threaten coastal communities, and an increase in the frequency of extreme weather events like droughts and floods. To mitigate these effects, the video emphasizes the importance of raising awareness and reducing individual carbon footprints through changed transportation and energy consumption habits.

I like the Inuit tribe’s point of view on the global warming topic which derives from multi-generational hunting observations. Basically, the sun used to rise at one mountain peak and set at another mountain peak. The sun would rise and fall in one hour during the late winter months allowing enough light to make a seal kill. Now the sun rises at another mountain peak and sets at another, and the sun is up for close to two hours. They call it a pole shift.

text: CO2 absorption of  terrestrial IR occurs at 15um wavelenth and follows "Beers Law" - college chemistry basics. The absorption is maximized, 99.9%, in under 10m from the earth's surface at CO2 concentration of just 200ppm or less.  Increasing CO2 does not increase absorption further, so there is no increase in IR energy absorption from the earth's surface with CO2 doubling. This is basic college chemistry and physics of the issue. The truth is that CO2 increase does not further add to the greenhouse effect  than it already does. There is a great series of short 4min lectures on YouTube about the details by Michel van Biezen.

text:
The truth is, we do not know precisely how much co2 is affecting the atmosphere. There are a lot of feedbacks both positive and negative. The problem is they change so often that it is impossible to say which is affecting the climate more. Take for example, clouds. Low level clouds are more transparent to infrared, meaning more escapes to space. At the same time, they are more reflective to sunlight. This means they cause the earth to cool. High level clouds are more reflective of infrared, meaning it causes the earth to stay warm. They also absorb more sunlight meaning it warms the atmosphere during the day. Water vapor also traps heat, unlike co2, which releases it as fast as it gets it in the form of infrared. So when a low energy co2 atom hits a water vapor that has high energy, it steals some of that energy and releases it immediately. Water vapor can catch the frequencies that co2 emits on, meaning it traps the heat.

What this means is if the majority of clouds are low level we have negative feedback to co2 increases. IF the clouds are high, we have positive feedback to co2 increases. The problem is that we have to model the atmosphere at about 1/1000 of what our best computers can do, to figure out the truth.

Since no computer can handle that, we use models that use a range of a variable called "climate sensitivity to co2 changes". They do the runs with the variable at one level, then again at a second level and so on. They often start at .5°c per doubling of co2 and go to 5°c per doubling of co2. The models at the low end are accurate, but can not predict a disaster. The ones at the high end do predict a disaster, but after a decade are considerably off. After 2 decades that are way off, and after 3 decades, they are outrageously off. YET they claim the top end is off and we will have a tipping point which switches the sensitivity from the low end to the high, but so far none of these tipping points have done anything.

text:
The Northern hemisphere is 33% greener than it was in 1900. Sea levels are rising at the same rate as they were in 1865. The hottest decade on record sine the ending of the "Little Ice Age", was the 1930s into the early 1940s. The earth cooled from from 1940 to 1979 by .3° C. 

There was no warming between 1998 and 2015, the Earth has not warmed since 2016. From February 2016 to February 2018, global average temperatures dropped by 0.56° C. The Earth was warmer, there was less sea ice, and the sea levels higher during the "Medieval Warm Period". 

2022 Summer Arctic sea extent was greater and the ice thicker, than it was in 2008. Antarctica has been gaining 112 billion tons of ice a year since 1992. Yet according to NASA 2022 was the sixth hottest year on record. There is no mass extinction event occurring due to climate change, the Polar Bear population is five times what it was in 1960, 
and at its highest ever recorded. 

In 2019 more children died from problems associated with obesity, than from malnutrition or starvation. In 2020 the United States produced 25% more food than it needed or could export. All major crop yields have increased three fold since the 1940s, due in no small part to increased CO2 in the atmosphere. CO2 is a greenhouse gas, however it has never been proven that concentrations of CO2 will cause warming, in fact we know from ice cores that CO2 concentration lags behind periods of rapid warming. 

The world population has exploded, however it was expected to top out at nine billion, and then begin decreasing. The reason being that as developing nations become more prosperous, they tend to have less children. By depriving developing nations access to cheap fuel, they are forced to burn wood and dung to cook, light and heat their homes, causing massive health problems, deforestation, and leading to a mass extinction event as they kill local wild life to sustain themselves with protein. Bottom line..... there is no "Climate Crisis"

Walk outside, breathe deep, and smile.
