text:
Some thoughts from an environmental economics Ph.D student:

First off, regarding the tipping point for electric vehicle uptake: One thing which you don't seem to mention (or that I at least missed) is the complementary role of the charging network infrastructure in the perceived value of acquiring an electric vehicle. In broad strokes, it's not very attractive to own an electric vehicle if there are a limited number of places to charge it, and similarly, it's not very attractive for a private company to build charging stations for electric vehicles if there aren't many electric vehicles on the road. In economic terms, we would say that the two things are complements. This means that governments could consider pursuing policies subsidizing the charging infrastructure in order to accelerate the uptake of electric vehicles by getting the charging infrastructure past it's "social tipping point"

Secondly, on the point of interventions, this is one point where environmental economists are often hesitant to recommend various mandates for technologies, instead preferring a direct tax on carbon emissions. Essentially, this has been known as the most cost effective intervention for 30+ years, and only in very specific circumstances are other policy interventions considered relevant. In fact, the idea of taxing harmful behavior goes all the way back to Arthur Pigou in 1920.

The reason why economists generally prefer taxing carbon over mandating certain solutions (such as a green hydrogen mandate) is because taxation is a more flexible form of regulation. In essence, by putting a price on carbon emissions, you are inherently disincentivizing people to invest in technologies with high carbon emissions (thereby contributing to the divestment tipping point) and incentivizing them to invest in low or zero carbon technologies (such as green hydrogen), but the key benefit of a tax is that the regulating agency doesn't have to specify which solution should be implemented, which allows private market forces to determine which options would be the most cost-efficient.

Finally, regarding who gets to make the decision, I agree that it's a complicated issue with few if any good solutions. In short, our global cross-country institutions as they are currently designed have relatively little power to implement supranational policies, with the EU being a notable exception. As such, for the time being, it will fall on state governments around the world to implement their own national policies. As you mention, policies to mitigate climate change will likely (in most cases almost certainly) have negative consequences for some parts of our society. This is, however, not a problem that is unique to climate change policy, although it is perhaps most notable in this case. 

I personally think that climate policy must have general support among the public if it is to have any chance of succeeding, since most of these policies will take time to produce their desired outcomes, and as such we should think about how we can best help those who stand to face increased hardship in the short term from our policies. Additionally, this also means that the public should ideally be at least moderately well-informed about the various impacts of policies, both the impacts it will have on them and their fellow citizens, in order to foster a healthy discussion regarding the policy implementation and how reasonable compensation can be ensured for those negatively affected by the policies.

This got a little long-winded, but I hope it's of use to at least some people

I think one crucial thing needs to be pointed out: Global warming is not impacted by what _proportion_ of human energy consumption comes from renewables. What matters is the absolut numbers, what amounts of GHGs are released. If we go from 0% renewables and 100% fossil, to 50% renewables and 50% fossil, but also at the same time doubble total energy consumption, there will be no change in emission of GHGs.
In the graphs shown in this video, the consumption of fossil fules are still on the rise, even if renewables are rising faster. As long as that trend dosen't change, we have not even started on the right track yet.
(Yes, the speed of growth will decrease before growth becomes negative, so a slowing in the speed of growth for fossil fuels is still a good sign, but it is not enough).

text:
It's nice to hear news like this, but we rarely address the elephants in the room, mostly - if we're getting more energy efficient, then how do we keep using more and more energy each year instead of less. A notable example is a new solar park in my city, which is great for green initiative, but at the same time city built that park it also installed street lights for several km on the road nearby that was doing just fine without them for 50+ years, technically those km's are now using about 25-30% of that solar park annual output. Same as EV's - it's great that we have a battery car option, but it would be a hella better if people could use public transport and be able to exist in the cities without needing to own a car in a first place (and I'm really specific about cities, rural areas are different topic).
We're kind of solving a huge problem, but we're ignoring our ever increasing, more often than not irrational, appetite for energy just because we can "use more now"...

And summers go longer without rain, noticeably hotter, and that combined with the heat sponge effect of the concrete jungle of cities, it's less and less possible to survive without an AC, even though a lot of people can't afford one.
I suspect Europe got hit among the hardest by the climate crisis.

text:
Politics is a real tangible part of this equation. If you support or try and fail to enact a good policy, you make your party vulnerable to attacks and fear mongering. In the American two party system, that directly means risking a Trump presidency. In essence, if you support policies that are too fast for voters, you risk consequences that are worse than if you did nothing at all.

There is no “I wish it wasn’t like this, I wish people saw the big picture” This is a fact of politics that we must deal with to solve the climate crisis.

This is why I support Biden’s subsidies for electric vehicles. I think subsidies as a whole are a great solution because that is essentially how gas vehicles work. We pollute the atmosphere and rivers without paying a dime for the damage. We are paying less for gas and gas vehicles than it truly costs. text:
In Germany where the disparity between the high ambition for renewable energies is in stark contrast to the potential for renewablea (low pontential for hydro power, too short coastlines for off-shore wind, very high energy use due to high level of industrialisation, high population desity, ...) there is constant and deep distrust towards greenifying the energy sector. The argument is that the CO2 economy we create(d) is essentially deindustrialising Germany and that this would be the true "left-green agenda". So their conclusion is that all the renewable energy ambition is for nothing, costs us wealth and just moves the industrial prodution to more CO2 intensive countries.

I see 2 possible outcomes in response to those critics:

1. We don't do anything, continue to fire coal for our energy, don't invest in low-carbon transport like trains, and to have short-term low energy cost and thus high-energy-industries stay in the country. But then other countries get so much renewables online that they have even cheaper energy and so we are hit with our pants down: No renewables AND no funds left for transition as the industry left to carbon-neutral countries with dirt cheap renewable energy.

2. We accept that right now transition will be expensive and sometimes painful (with not a small amount of medium-sized businesses going out of business, which specialized on fossil tech). But in the carbon neutral future, which we then reached, we can bring back industries and their jobs, have cheap energy, have the funds to further invest in infrastructure and keep the country competetive on the world market.

As we are around the peak of the pain-curve in the transition (high energy cost DUE to high invest in energy infrastructure AND subsidizing fossil in parallel to keep the country going) it is getting more and more difficult to convince people of the vision in point 2 ... election results in the german states are showing dramatic shifts towards populist parties (right and left) leaning heavily against the painful transition to electrification and renewable energies.

So the end of the video asks who should decide. The question is: will people who do the (right now) uncomfortable decissions be in power when it comes to putting them in place or enforce them?

text:
As an Energy Engineer who works in the Nuclear industry I really appreciate this video; I felt the same about being lied to by political groups about Nuclear when I learned about it.

However, I don't think you should discount Carbon Capture and Storage; and more rigorous cap and trade. If you put that cap low, the developed country needs to reduce its emissions somehow, CCS works quite well in conjunction with that and overall allows approaching closer to net zero.

C&T I think works better when restricted to necessary industries that can't avoid a lot of their emissions, particularly heavy industry: concrete and steel production for example... Even a 10% (a low estimate) capture of their emissions would significantly affect countries totals.

Renewables also obviously play a very important role, especially alternative storage technologies; solar heat storage (using a salt based steam cycle) and pumped water storage paired with wind; both already exist and are fairly effective at reducing the huge power variations. 

Older technologies factor into that as well, like tidal barrages and hydroelectric which was touched on briefly, if it goes towards reducing dependence on fossil fuel emissions go for it; same goes for hydrogen electrolysis and fuel cells.

Overall the goal is in the future to have the vast majority of power come from renewable sources; wind farms, solar plants and hydroelectric dams, (hydrogen and tidal/wave power too maybe); but as you mention a strong base will be needed to support that and Nuclear can fill that niche and provide that...

The net emissions from nuclear are astonishingly low (including the whole mining to disposing fuel cycle) and Fusion and GenIV reactors show a lot of promise for deployment in 10-20 years... It also has one of the best work-in:work-out ratios of any energy source.

text:
I feel like the rationale behind Cap and Trade has been presented a little "unfairly":
First of all, it is a system designed to set incentives to reduce your emissions. Some firms have cheaper options than others for reducing their carbon footprint. By being able to trade the "carbon credits", a market price forms and "determines" which firms will sell their credits and which will buy some, namely those will sell who can reduce, say, 1 unit of emissions for cheaper than that market price of credit. Therefore, those firms with cheap reduction opportunities reduce first. The trick here is to set the amount of "carbon credit" just so that reduction HAS to happen: if you just hand out as much credit as your economy happens to blow out, nothing will happen obviously.

Second, if nothing changed after that, the situation would not become better, of course. However, the amount of carbon credit is reduced every period by the government. This could happen through simple collection of some of that credit, or (what would be more popular with most industries and therefore politically more viable) via the government buying credit back at market prices (or lower, if that could be enforced...): Point is, without the government regularly and rapidly reducing the amount of available carbon credit, this system would just be like you presented it: not doing anything! However, if a government would follow through with the system, this could contribute to the reduction of emissions.
Of course, there are flaws with this approach: The actual production of emissions would have to be measured and non-compliance would have to be punished, which would add costs for enforcement. But honestly, so does every regulation. Also, the fact that by buying back carbon credit, you give those firms financial aid which have the hardest time reducing their carbon footprint, might in practice lead to financial support for the worst offenders when it comes to climate change, which might not sit right with activists. Also, when looking at actual real world attempts at introducing such a system like in the EU's case, the system failed because the amount of certificates ("credit") were not reduced over time. After every cheap measurement had been implemented, the amount of certified pollution and actual emissions about matched, which let their market price plummet. ALSO, the system contained legal loopholes, allowing firms to buy certificates and then write them off as lost profit and receive a tax cut WHICH OBVIOUSLY SHOULDN'T HAPPEN. Take-away here is, that it might be quite tricky to actually implement such a system.
This is also why I put "unfairly" in the first sentence: In theory, Cap and Trade can contribute a lot, in practice though it has not proved itself useful yet as far as I know.

So, yeah, long story short, I know that you cannot attribute a huge part of your video to such a minor detail, but I think, as I was here anyway, I could contribute my two cents to it. Cheers whoever actually read through this! Feel free to correct me on anything, I wrote mostly from memory and what I've learned in several courses over the years.

P.S.: What I didn't mention: Such a system of course requires also harsh financial punishments for firms which do not comply with their carbon limits. These fines would have to be costly enough to make reductions worth it (in the long run). Otherwise, why reduce your emissions when the penalty is cheaper?

text:
Thanks, for another great, informative  and thought provoking video! There are some problems with nuclear that didn't get mentioned, such as proliferation. Uranium enrichment is a process that increases the relative amount of U-235 isotope that is useful in the fission process, however this same process can be used to create weapons grade material. An intentional attack using nuclear weapons is a scary thought which I think can put some people off the idea.

 Unfortunately, I believe some would take advantage and play on those fears for their own agenda and the threat can be exaggerated (eg check out John Oliver, Iran Deal). Weapons grade uranium must be enriched to a much higher level (>90% instead of 3-20%) which is a significant difference, international enrichment facilities can be used to supply fuel to nation states without allowing them access to the enrichment technology and since you were talking mainly about US climate policy and the US already has plenty of nukes, there's not as much of an argument here against more nuclear plants in the US.

 Another issue with nuclear power is the waste material, it is, in ways, much more hazardous than green house gases. Atmospheric CO2 levels have a bit to go before it become harmful for humans to breathe, but touching or just being near radioactive material can cause radiation sickness. Of course this ignores the larger impact of heightened CO2 levels in the atmosphere such as rising tides / displaced residents / fish etc, but where CO2 can be reduced from the atmosphere with forestation efforts, a small, but arguably significant, amount (~3%) of waste is deemed High Level Waste, and needs to be disposed underground for thousands of years.

 This is a very small amount by volume when bearing in mind nuclear already has a brilliantly low waste output rate, and, when handled properly with modern technology and effective storage and disposal techniques, the risk is substantially mitigated to the point that it is, as you say, significantly less dangerous that other forms of power generation. It is also true that other industries create dangerous pollutants that can last indefinitely such as cadmium and mercury.

 I think it definitely seems like Nuclear would be great for buying us a bunch more time at least, which is what we desperately need right now, but I'd be interested if anyone had any thoughts on these talking points and whether they hold water?

text:
Overall, a very good video. You made a lot of salient points on nuclear energy, and various emission-limiting policies (such as cap-and-trade). I also appreciated your criticism of the "overpopulation" argument.

However, there is one point where you made a mistake, and that's on the viability of wind & solar. For one, those farms do not "only last a few years before they need to be replaced." The average lifespan of solar panels - and solar farms - that are in current production is around 20 to 30 years. Wind turbines do require more regular maintenance, but they, too, last quite a bit longer than "a few years." Similarly, they amount of toxic byproducts produced by them is negligible, and solar panels in particular are between 90 to 97% recyclable at end-of-life. 

And finally, about energy storage. We've already negated the "unreliability" of wind & solar with broad distributed energy storage methods. I'm surprised you've never touched on liquid air/cryogenic energy storage: it's scales extremely well, is cheap, and environmentally friendly, while relying on very well-founded technology, and no special materials. This more than makes up for on-demand energy needs. The problem here isn't a technological one, or even economic: it's a matter of political will.

text:
Gotta be honest, you almost lost me at the beginning, but I do wholeheartedly support your conclusions. You didn't pull any punches when talking about nuclear, coal, wind, or solar, and while you did underplay just how big a step up natural gas is from coal (I mean, even petroleum products are a massive step up from coal, it's not a hard thing to top), you clearly understand the most important things about this debate. As an engineer, I can wholeheartedly support your message.

All that said I have serious reservations about climate science (whether we call it global warming or climate change), because while it is happening there are heaps of money being used by unscrupulous actors to influence the science. 'Carbon colonialism' isn't just an international thing, big companies in the US have a far easier time meeting emissions standards than small ones, and can effectively choke out competition by lobbying for more stringent regulations. Climate alarmism helps big businesses to get these standards passed, and not coincidentally many of these studies are sponsored by large companies. Combine this obvious money trail with the wildly disparate figures from so many of these studies, and I think it's fair to be skeptical.

You also have a very Eurocentric and deterministic view of industrialization, one that simply put does not conform to reality. Modern developing countries do not need to go through the same industrial revolution that the US and Europe did, because they have access to a far greater base of knowledge and technology that we did during our industrial revolution. More importantly, that's not what they're doing anyways. China and India aren't polluting to provide for the needs of their people, they're doing it to provide low cost consumer goods to Europe and the US. They won't stop ramping up production when their pollution level allows them to reach western standards of living, they'll stop when the demand in western countries for cheap goods is met.

This is actually the most insidious part of the 'carbon colonialism' you mentioned. People in China and India are suffering from the environmental and health effects of shouldering what would normally be OUR pollution, and the wealth generated from all this pollution remains mostly concentrated in the hands of the wealthy elite. I'm sure you don't support trickle down economics, so why you seem to think the best way to help the lower classes in China and India is to allow their corporations to pollute more so they can make more money is beyond me. Fact is that those carbon emissions are killing them in droves, and you should be just as worried about them polluting over there for their sake as you are about it for ours.


Good video, but you're missing some major parts of the puzzle. Follow the money.

Was with you until you said that renewables would likely never meet energy demands. What is your basis for this? You also say that they usually need to be supplemented by coal or natural gas. I do agree with your conclusions about biomass, yes it's not viable solution. I think this is becoming consensus in the scientific community already, and I doubt you will find many scientists who will go to bat for biomass as the saviour.

To be clear, I am pro-nuclear, I think countries like France, Belgium, Slovakia have benefitted enormously from their nuclear programmes. And yes, the issue of nuclear waste has been massively blown out of proportion of decades. Where I think you got this wrong though was glossing of Chernobyl, and only mentioning Fukushima in passing. Okay, more people have likely died as a result of fossil fuels, but how many people have been struck by a wind turbine blade? Maybe some of the processes involved in refining materials for solar panels, collectors, and turbines produce air pollution. But I nuclear power has killed more, of that I'm sure. The 'propaganda number' for deaths due to nuclear sits wrong with me as well. No one can know how many people have been killed by nuclear, in the same way you can't know how many have died of fossil fuels. Personally, my girlfriend and many of her friends have thyroid problems as a direct result of Chernobyl (born in Poland 1986). Nuclear disasters effect vast vast numbers of people, and it's not something that ended with Chernobyl, and it won't end with Fukashima.

I think there is a general American bias towards nuclear power emerging. Many people are just realising all lies that they've been told about it, and now believe it to be the saviour. While in Europe (many countries intend to phase out the reactors), and soon probably in Japan (most of there reactors have been shut down for now), where we have felt the effects of nuclear disasters first hand, there is a more nuanced understanding that Nuclear power is deeply deeply flawed.

@Knowing Better: Re: Nuclear Power - You chose to overlook a number of points:
(1) The world is not "America". Nuclear power plants in Venezuela, Iran, Iraq, North Korea, Afghanistan, Sudan, South Sudan, Somalia, Libya, Syria? (Just to name a few obvious ones.)
(2) Nuclear fuel is a limited resource. Uranium at current levels of consumption will last quite a while, but this is with nuclear power plants producing just about 14% of global electricity at current consumption levels, with projections being that total electricity consumption might double until 2050. At 100% Nuclear Uranium reserves would last for about 10 years without using breeder reactors. About these I refer back to (1) ... global proliferation of Plutonium production sounds like a rather bad idea to me.
(3) The oft proposed solution for (2) of using Thorium has never even seen a successful prototype. In fact the only few prototypes I know of all failed spectacularly after spending billions on them. Taking into account the lessons learned then, getting from the draft stage to a working plant will still take at best ten years.
(4) Building nuclear power plants takes an average of ~10 years from first concrete to first power, and that is including China that is more efficient at building these by nature of being a dictatorship. On top to number excludes the planning stage, so you'd have to add another five years or so, at the least.
(5) No company in the world would currently be able to build enough reactors in parallel. The companies that historically built most of the existing plants (GE, Siemens, Areva) have more or less shut down the relevant divisions. Not sure about Russian companies, but Soviet tech doesn't sound like a good idea. And the Chinese? Well, for once they are quite busy with projects in their own country, and then there's the matter of geopolitics, again ... 

Ramping that back up to the necessary capacity would probably take another ten years or so ... nuclear engineers don't grow on trees.

(6) Running nuclear plants would be a problem in at least half the world. It requires qualified personnel, supply chains, expensive maintenance etc. Someone once suggested to me that this could be solved by the companies building the plants contracting to run them, too, but this kind of Neo-colonialism wouldn't go down too well nowadays, either.



I agree, fifteen or twenty years ago nuclear (fission) might have been an option from a tech point of view, at least as a bridge technology, but the fact is it wouldn't have been feasible politically. Now is simply too late.

It's also a target for terrorist attacks. I know it has never happened, and I know how those reactors are constructed, but the potential is there and the consequences would be devastating as we all should know.
The construction of reactors at least in the USA has always gone over budget big time.
And just like people predict peak oil in a few decades, peak uranium isn't too far over the horizon. Yes, resource peaks are real. If you don't think so you don't understand what it is.

I think wind is going to be the preferred energy source for the next 1,000 years or however long it takes for us to not rely on Earth. In some sci-fi reality in the future everything is probably gonna be nuclear. But while we're stuck on Earth wind is very sustainable. If it weren't for those damn birds killing themselves.

text:
Some Al Gore predictions that didn’t come to pass:

❄️ 1. “No more snows of Kilimanjaro within a decade”

In An Inconvenient Truth (2006), Gore stated: “within the decade there will be no more snows of Kilimanjaro.” Scientifically, this referred to an expectation that Kilimanjaro’s ice cap would vanish by around 2016. However, while Kilimanjaro’s glacier has indeed shrunk significantly over the past century, the ice has persisted past that timeline, contrary to the exact wording of Gore’s prediction.

🧊 2. Arctic summer ice free by 2016 (75% probability)

At the 2009 Copenhagen Climate Conference, Gore cited research suggesting a 75% chance that the entire north polar ice cap could be completely ice‑free during some summer months within five to seven years, aiming for roughly 2014–2016. As of today (mid‑2025), Arctic sea ice still persists in summer (although reduced compared to past decades), so the prediction did not come true within that timeframe￼.

text: Brilliant video! Education is significant, and the fight against all those who prevent girls from going to schools and attaining quality education is crucial. Malala has given voice to a very important matter. However, I want to bring the attention of all the policy-makers and decision-makers to much deeper roots of the issue. The main causes of the inequalities are embedded in the religious, cultural, traditional, patriarchal mindsets of the people, particularly in rural areas of countries like Pakistan. These mindsets prevent women from their right to education, right to marry, right to health, and right to live. I want to bring attention to this question; how to change this social fabric that continuously undermines women and their human rights? Providing access to schools, building more schools, and creating opportunities are but a half solutions to a big problem. My question is, even if there are more schools and opportunities and safe access to those opportunities, how many of our women will or will be allowed by their families to shed the shackles of their traditions and step out of their patriarchal household setting to avail those opportunities?

text: With climate change, only showing the "global" CO2 emissions is not good enough. It is important to be transparent about which nations are the LEADING emitters of GHG and holding THEM accountable. You cannot say we need to reduce "global GHG emissions" yet we know that if most Global North countries can reduce their emissions by more than half, is really what we need to change the current trajectory of climate change. But that won't happen, because it is a far more complex issue. We can plant trees all we want but for as long as the world's largest polluters do not change, this cause will be more about adapting than mitigating. I am so tired of hearing "global" this "global" that. NO! We KNOW the largest polluters. The narrative should be holding them accountable.
